title: Bind mounting /dev to another location when rootless silently fails and creates only empty files
body:
### Issue Description

When running a bind mount against /dev in a rootless container, the resulting bind mount is filled with empty files instead of ie character devices.

### Steps to reproduce the issue

Steps to reproduce the issue
1. `podman run -it --privileged docker.io/rockylinux/rockylinux:9 /bin/bash`
2. `mkdir /dev2`
3. `mount -n --bind /dev /dev2`
4. `ls -l /dev2/urandom` (empty regular file)


### Describe the results you received

I am seeing only empty regular files in the bind mount.

### Describe the results you expected

I expected a proper bind mount like what happens in a rootful container, or at least a failure message if this is not possible.

### podman info output

```yaml
Podman 5.7.0 NixOS unstable
```

### Podman in a container

No

### Privileged Or Rootless

Rootless

### Upstream Latest Release

Yes

### Additional environment details

Additional environment details

### Additional information

Additional information like issue happens only occasionally or issue happens with a particular architecture or on a particular setting

comments.body:
Hey, can you provide more info to the reproducer?

>    podman run -it --privileged docker.io/library/rocky:9.2 /bin/bash
>    Error: unable to copy from source ...

Are you pulling `docker.io/library/rockylinux:9.2`? There is no image for `rocky`

Assuming you did, I have issues with further steps too. For example, `dev` exists already in the container, so I am missing something.

```
[root@fbea6451b259 /]# mkdir dev
mkdir: cannot create directory ‘dev’: File exists
```
@inknos I have updated the instructions, please try now.

ok, clear now.

I am not sure `--bind` is the right option, `--rbind` might do what you want. but I am not sure of the whys and I believe it has to do with how podman handle recursive mounts. maybe @giuseppe could help me here?
Note that this does work correctly in a rootful container.

Well rootless and rooful need different behavior. Rootful can create devices via mknod so all the devices are actually created again in the container mount ns. Rootless cannot do that so we opted to bind mount each device file instead.

Because each file is their own mount would would have to bind mount recursive via --rbind as pointed out.

Just compare the output of mount between the two. The empty files happen because in order to bind mount to a target it must be an existing inode so all bind mount targets that do not exists will be created as empty file hence you see only empty files as you do not keep the mounts recursive.

I do not know the history of why we mount each file one by one as rootless in this case and not just the full /dev but I guess there is a reason that @giuseppe might know

rootless cannot create device nodes, so it bind mounts the devices.

If you use a bind you are only bind mounting the `/dev` directory without any of the other mounts on the top.

So you need `rbind` to have a recursive mount of all the devices
